item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This certainly puts the lie to the claim that articles all have to be long and complicated to be successful these days. Very clever and to the point; nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, there are do many articles on the site nowadays… You can't be blamed for missing one. And while the idea behind both article are the same, tour exécution is far more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…He was compared to his brother.

Now nothing can ever compare him to anything, ever again.

Absolutely chilling, in a tragic way. It already had my +1, but I had to come back and comment after it snapped into focus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! Yep, pretty much! No idea that one existed, but it's far, far too late to do anything about it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may have musunderstood, but isn't this very similar to SCP-1144
? They're different in their approach, but I feel like the gimmick is exactly the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article seems like one that was written in a rush

I can guarantee you that, like most of my articles, this was a long time in the making.

the sole intention of introducing a concept that would be used in a future article

I only included the DoMC because I wanted to draw more attention to the excellent scip DarkStuff wrote1, and to cement the department's core themes and ideas (which I am not wholly responsible for, but still very much enjoy).

the writing style is awkward

Could you elaborate on this, please? "Awkward" is a term that makes it essentially impossible for me to correct your criticisms in this article, or take them on board for future works.

Footnotes
1. Also, worth noting that this isn't the first DoMC article, as you seem to be implying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh SHIT, I didn't expect it to get so dark so quickly.

Honestly, the horror of it comes from the fact that it's obscured and nobody knows what it is, but it's implied to be Fisk's dead body. The idea of someone entering the bathroom, seeing the dead body, and being completely unable to describe it, along with the censored image…

It kind of reminds me of "The Thing on the Doorstep", except that it's "The Thing in the Bathtub". The horror aspect of it is in the context surrounding it.

item.title: Lang"dado"n Pisk??
item.content:encoded: Langdon is seemingly infinitely 𝒩, and in dado's Forgetti Medicine Adventure, Dado is implied to be the exact anomalous opposite of this SCP. Maybe "dado" is the name he's given himself to escape his Pisk legacy. What if… dado sapped the 𝒩-ergy from Damien, or as dado calls him, 'dado nefarious dark brother "bado"'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article seems like one that was written in a rush, made with the sole intention of introducing a concept that would be used in a future article. As such, it really cannot stand as its own SCP. Beyond that, the writing style is awkward, and the procedures seem out of character for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was unsure how to feel about it first, but once I understood the concept I was interested in reading more of it. Very well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry, but I'm just really confused by this "n"-ness. The concept would be really interesting if I actually understood it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people have said that 𝒩ness could be from Pisk’s perspective, however:

For the purposes of determining how 𝒩 various objects are in relation to the anomaly, Balance.aic2 has been designed to compare live observation of SCP-4517 with assorted photographs, videos, and textual descriptions.

This (to me at least) implies that 𝒩ness can be inferred from pictures of the skip in relation to what is being tested for 𝒩ness, and that this comparison can be done by a machine. (I.E., not from the perspective of Pisk)

I.e., 𝒩ness is a property/quality that doesn’t require the perspective of a specific person, but just a perspective.

Current SCP-J-1132 instance
- Title for hiding sample text

so, i think that 𝒩ness is a representation of the difference in amount of ontological domains/accidental properties in relation to The skip, which has zero “domains” that it takes part in.

Not as in amount of diffrences, but in amount of domains that can be compared To each-other.

However, I feel that this clashes with the fact that it does have essential properties that can be compared. So, perhaps the skip does have ontological domains, it’s just that it has the least amount of them? And as such serves as the “bottom value” of 𝒩ness despite it having “some” properties???

May I consult with the head of the DoMc regarding nuances of 𝒩ness?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you think so! The Semantic Dissociator — along with SCP-2719 — was one of my biggest inspirations writing this, and it remains one of my favourite articles to-date. Writing something thematically similar but different and interesting enough to stand alone was basically my entire goal, and I'm very pleased to see that it worked!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I look forward to seeing them :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They may seem obvious to us, but that's only because our brains are hardwired to recognize those sorts of things.

Yep! And this is the reason they used an artificial intelligence to recognise it, rather than a simpler, cheaper, more efficient piece of software. The whole anomaly hinges on the idea that concepts humans create — death, inside and outside, line-of-sight, memory, names and identifiers — have tangible, real-world meaning. It's actually a core premise of the Foundationverse and anomalies as a whole, but one that's often not addressed: I wanted to write a thing that explore the logical conclusion of something that's basically been taken for granted for over a decade ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The qualities you mentioned, "how many of a thing there are", "what colour is this thing", are not in fact obvious. They may seem obvious to us, but that's only because our brains are hardwired to recognize those sorts of things. In reality, however, determining these qualities is incredibly difficult, which is why computer programs designed to recognize these sorts of things are so difficult to make.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting, my own theory is:

+ show block
– hide block

That the numbers are how highly 𝒩 the contents of the bathtub held something in esteem. An old toy is more dear than a new one, and the brother is so highly valued he broke the system.

Meanwhile, Damien valued himself at 0. That he may be the reason why he was found in the bathtub…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I thought the guy had been killed by his brother and the quantity was just a vector from victim to murderer. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely love this. We need more content here thats ontological in nature! not everything is a cognitohazard or some kind of meme/concept. Some things just exist in way we are not used to and very uncomfortable with!

I had a couple planned along these lines, that might still get written at some point, something I'm very excited about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was short, but ok, I just have to say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a lot like SCP-1539. But it works, and I like it.

When I understood1 what those numbers meant, goosebumps, just goosebumps. The 𝒩ness of the old stuffed toy and Landon Pisk was really sad.

SPOILERS  
 Possibly wrong so meh 

I think what the numbers mean is that it signifies how close/far SCP-4517 is to the entity it is being compared to. Like how the 𝒩ness of the researchers are in the millions while the D-personnel is in the thousands meaning the value of SCP-4517 is closer to being a D-personnel rather than being a Researcher. The 𝒩ness of Damien Pisk being 0 means you now know who/what is in the bathtub and the 𝒩ness Langdon Pisk being COMPARISON REJECTED; INTEGER OVERFLOW means you now know why the thing/object in the bathtub is there now.

Footnotes
1. At least I think I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that a 15 year old plushie was thousands of times more 𝒩 than a new one made me kind of emotional. 𝒩ness is the ability of something to be described. That 2,300 𝒩 represents all the happy memories.

The comparison between Damien and Langdon is also fascinating. Someone earlier in the thread mentioned that Damien himself could be the skip, and that he may have felt his brother was worth so much more than him. 4517 is also person-sized. This would strongly imply that 𝒩ness is itself a subjective measure from the perspective of Damien! This also makes me emotional, because that implies that the stuffed animal recovered from the apartment was HIS, and contained all HIS memories with it in its 𝒩 value.

This skip is an incredible example of emotional storytelling using raw data and bizarre metaphysics. This article makes me feel genuinely sad and wistful for the characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a soft spot for SCPs conceptual SCPs. It's not enough to break the laws of physics, you have to break metaphysics too.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's my explanation…

+ Show stuff
- Hide stuff

The effect here is neither memetic nor antimemetic; it is, in fact ontological. They can't recognise SCP-4517 as Pisk because the anomaly does not possess that quality any more — it's not hiding it, it's just not there. It's not an issue that's in our heads, it's an issue that's in the world. It's the same problem with 𝒩ness: it is fundamentally not any of those concepts you mentioned. It might have been, at one point, but not any more. The Foundation could very well provide details about what the two (4517 and 𝒩ness) are, but they would (by definition) be inaccurate. And since the ontological anomaly is structured in such a way that the absence and presence of qualities are obvious to the observer, it would seem foolish (to them, if not to us) to write about something that is patently not true.

The metric of comparison is also not memetic, at least at an anomalous level (ignoring the fact that it's anomalous in the first place). It's just… obvious. In the same way that "how many of a thing there are" or "what colour is this thing" could be fairly easily quantified through observation, so can "how 𝒩 an object is". The AIC is not affected by some strange memetic effect; it's just a neural net doing the comparisons that we don't have time to do ourselves. The Foundation wouldn't be in trouble here if SCP-4517 had ill intentions, because that would need to be a concept unaffected by the anomaly in order for the object to have it. If it was unaffected by the anomaly, the Foundation would be able to tell, and they'd up security; as it is, they know it can't have any intentions at all, and so they're comfortable locking it up where it was found and increasing surveillance of the area.

Basically, in my canon/headcanon version of the Foundation, they don't immediately jump to antimemetics as an explanation, because actual antimemetic anomalies are incredibly rare (from their perspective, anyway; there's a whole division that means they'll never find out how plentiful they really are). This is a relatively small Foundation (still gigantic by real-life standards, but maybe not as puppeteering as some versions) that has a fairly good idea of what's going on. Trust the DoMC; they're experts after all!

Also, this is why I said to consult the DoMC head for the details of the nuance of the property. All this explanation would have doubled the article's length :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some observations:

There's an antimemetic element at play here. First, 𝒩ness itself can't be described. The obvious thing to do when you want to describe a factor is to test its correlations with other known factors and give the correlation coefficients, yet the Foundation could not do that (the most obvious factors being "intelligence", "humanity", and "value assigned by society"). Second, the obvious hypothesis that Damien Pisk is SCP-4517 is not stated, even though the data is extremely suggestive of that. Third, the extreme danger of an antimemetic hazard is ignored, the entity is classified as "Safe", and tenants are allowed to remain in the building.

There is also a memetic element that promotes the idea of 𝒩ness. There is no explanation of how they came up with this metric - "similarity to SCP-4517" is not a sufficient description, there are many ways to be similar to SCP-4517, for instance, having a similar mass. There was an additional influence above and beyond SCP-4517 merely cutting off avenues of measuring it. They came up with it because that's how SCP-4517 *wanted* them to measure it.

Also, the Foundation's countermemetic strategies *suck*. Not only are all its countermemetic defenses penetrated to full strategic depth with nobody even having a clue what just happened, its AIs are revealed to also be vulnerable. The only thing that saves the world is that SCP-4517 had no ill intentions in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this. This is an ideal example of test log based storytelling, and the amount of information conveyed in the short final two tests is extremely impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you thought so! I was a bit worried writing this one, to be honest — it's quite similar to a whole bunch of stuff (like DarkStuff's DoMC article, the semantic dissociator, and UNDEFINED). I'm very pleased that you thought it stood out enough from the crowd :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One can certainly argue that "objective" is a concept that only exists from the human perspective, but then you're forced to apply that same concept to everything. SCP-2935 doesn't work because death isn't a discrete, meaningful state. SCP-173 doesn't work because 'line of sight' doesn't have concrete definition at the reductionist level. The SCP universe operates, and will continue to operate, at a level where human-created concepts have real physical meaning, and this article is no exception.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

Objective isn't really a thing; humans just like to pretend. And you can't have it be indescribable and then say "but it's also not indescribable", because clearly it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is possibly the only time that Philosophy of Language class I took in college has ever come in handy, and I'm still not sure I "get it". I'll get to the one linked in the article later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been busy with other stuff for the last week or so, so I hadn't realised you'd written this. Good stuff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s pretty much the conclusion I came to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoiler
– hide block

Is this implying that Damien Pisk, the previous apartment resident, is dead in the bathtub and producing the anomaly due to his inherent uselessness, especially in comparison to his brother's success, and that he died from suicide?

Or am I reading way too into a single test log entry? In either case, that's an upvote :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like there have been an increasing number of antimeme-type infohazards in Series V so far. The relative comparison hook sets this apart enough to make it worth for me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yayy! The second in the planned Department of Miscommunications trilogy! I like the puzzle this presents, [spoilers ahead…] and I’m still trying to figure out whose perspective N is from! Good job my man. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually really good. I feel that I have a bit of a better grasp on what n-ness actually is than was intended, but it was still really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to DarkStuff and Mortos, who were part of the original brainstorming for this! DarkStuff's contribution to the Department is linked in the footnote, and if Mortos ever writes his, you can be sure I'll be adding it as well! Thanks also to the people who looked this over in chat — there were unfortunately far too many of you to properly credit.

Anyway, enjoy! Spoilers will not be provided!

Image: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Clawfoot_bathtub.jpg
